items.foco: Fisher scale and risk of vasospasm
items.title: Fisher scale and vasospasm
items.contentHtml:
Fisher Scale

  • Fisher grade I: no visible blood; low risk of vasospasm.
  • Fisher grade II: diffuse blood or layer <1 mm; intermediate risk.
  • Fisher grade III: clot or layer >1 mm; high risk of vasospasm.
  • Fisher grade IV: intraventricular or parenchymal hemorrhage with diffuse blood; very high risk.
  • The modified Fisher scale (with thickness and presence of IVH) best predicts vasospasm. Peak vasospasm occurs between days 3-14.

items.title: Cushing's triad
items.contentHtml:
Cushing's triad

  • Hypertension (with wide pulse pressure), bradycardia and irregular breathing (Cheyne-Stokes or ataxic pattern).
  • It is a late sign of severe intracranial hypertension and impending herniation.
  • Pathophysiology: ischemia of the vasomotor center of the medulla oblongata due to compression.
  • The presence of the triad requires aggressive management: head elevation 30°, hyperventilation (PaCO2 30-35 mmHg) and mannitol (0.25-1 g/kg) or hypertonic saline solution.

items.foco: Digital subtraction angiography is gold standard
items.title: Gold standard angiography
items.contentHtml:
Digital subtraction angiography (DSA)

  • Gold standard for the diagnosis of cerebral aneurysms in subarachnoid hemorrhage.
  • It allows identifying aneurysms <3 mm, assessing vasospasm and planning endovascular or surgical treatment.
  • Non-invasive alternatives: Angio-computed tomography (sensitivity >95% for aneurysms >3 mm) and Angio-magnetic resonance imaging.
  • ASD has a risk of complications (dissection, embolism, contrast nephropathy), but it is irreplaceable if there is diagnostic or treatment doubt.

items.foco: Sudden thunderclap headache is diagnostic key
items.title: Sudden thunderclap headache
items.contentHtml:
Sudden thunderclap headache

  • Diagnostic key for subarachnoid hemorrhage: sudden, intense onset headache (the worst in life), which reaches maximum intensity in <1 minute.
  • It is associated with nausea, vomiting, neck stiffness, photophobia and loss of consciousness.
  • Differential diagnosis: venous thrombosis brain, arterial dissection, reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome.
  • In case of thunderclap headache, perform computed tomography of the head without contrast (sensitivity >95% in the first 6 hours); if negative, lumbar puncture to look for xanthochromia.

items.foco: Nimodipine to prevent vasospasm in SAH
items.title: Nimodipine in HSA
items.contentHtml:
Nimodipine

  • Nimodipine 60 mg orally every 4 hours for 21 days (or 0.5-1 mg/h intravenous if oral administration is not possible) is the only drug that has been shown to reduce the poor outcome due to vasospasm in subarachnoid hemorrhage.
  • Mechanism: calcium channel blocker type L, with selective effect on cerebral arteries.
  • Does not prevent angiographic vasospasm, but improves neurological prognosis.
  • Monitor hypotension (main adverse effect).

items.foco: Acute vs chronic subdural hematoma on computed tomography
items.title: Acute vs chronic subdural
items.contentHtml:
Acute vs. chronic subdural hematoma on computed tomography

  • Acute subdural hematoma: hyperdense (white), concave crescent shape, does not cross the falx or tentorium, associated with cerebral contusion.
  • Chronic subdural hematoma: hypodense (dark gray or isodense), may have levels, is organized with membranes; It occurs in the elderly or alcoholics.
  • The chronic one may be isodense to the parenchyma, requiring contrast or magnetic resonance imaging to differentiate it.
  • The midline shift >5 mm is a criterion for urgent surgical evacuation in acute cases.

items.title: Epidural tear sign
items.contentHtml:
Teardrop sign

  • Teardrop sign on computed tomography: biconvex epidural hematoma (in a biconvex lens) that does not cross the cranial sutures, typically due to rupture of the middle meningeal artery.
  • Associated with temporal bone fracture; may present the lucid interval (transient recovery followed by deterioration).
  • Urgent surgical evacuation is required if thickness >1.5 cm or midline shift >5 mm.
  • The teardrop sign describes the morphology of the collection, not the shape of the intra-axial hemorrhage.

items.title: Decompressive craniectomy window
items.contentHtml:
Therapeutic window for decompressive craniectomy

  • Indicated in refractory intracranial hypertension (ICP >20 mmHg persistent despite 1st line measures).
  • The ideal therapeutic window is within the first 24-48 hours of the onset of refractory ICH; delay >48h is associated with a worse prognosis.
  • Perform wide decompressive craniectomy (hemicraniectomy) with duroplasty.
  • Relative contraindication: irreversible brain stem injury or uncorrectable coagulopathy.

items.foco: Lumbar puncture contraindicated if mass effect
items.title: Contraindicated LP with mass effect
items.contentHtml:
Lumbar puncture contraindicated

  • Absolute contraindication for lumbar puncture: evidence of mass effect on computed tomography (midline shift, herniation, obstructive hydrocephalus) or space-occupying lesion.
  • Risk: cerebral herniation (transtentorial or tonsillar) due to gradient of pressure.
  • If SAH is suspected with inconclusive computed tomography, perform LP only if non-contrast computed tomography shows absence of mass effect.
  • LP should measure opening pressure and look for xanthochromia (yellow supernatant after centrifuge).

items.foco: Hunt and Hess classification in HSA
items.title: Hunt and Hess Classification
items.contentHtml:
Hunt and Hess Classification

  • Grade I: asymptomatic or mild headache.
  • Grade II: moderate headache, stiff neck, no focal deficit except cranial nerve palsy.
  • Grade III: confusion, somnolence, mild focal deficit.
  • Grade IV: stupor, moderate hemiparesis, decerebrate rigidity.
  • Grade V: deep coma, decerebrate rigidity, signs of herniation.
  • Used to predict surgical prognosis; grades I-III are candidates for early intervention; Grades intravenous-V have a worse prognosis.

items.foco: Refractory intracranial hypertension managed with sedation
items.title: Management of refractory ICH with sedation
items.contentHtml:
Refractory intracranial hypertension

  • First line: head elevation 30°, mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg intravenous, hypertonic saline solution 3% (bolus 250 mL), mild hyperventilation (PaCO2 30-35 mmHg).
  • If refractory (ICP >20 mmHg despite the above): sedation with propofol or midazolam, plus neuromuscular blockade (cisatracurium).
  • 3rd line options: barbiturates (thiopental in barbiturate coma), therapeutic hypothermia (32-34°C), decompressive craniectomy.
  • Monitor with ICP and maintain cerebral perfusion pressure >60 mmHg.

items.foco: Maximum rebleeding in first 24 hours in SAH
items.title: Rebleeding in HSA first 24 hours
items.contentHtml:
Rebleeding in SAH

  • The peak of rebleeding occurs in the first 24 hours after the initial subarachnoid hemorrhage, with a cumulative incidence of 4-14% in that period.
  • Risk factors: uncontrolled arterial hypertension, large aneurysm (>10 mm), poor clinical grade (Hunt and Hess intravenous-V).
  • Prevention: strict control of BP (systolic pressure <160 mmHg), nimodipine and early treatment of the aneurysm (clipping or embolization) in the first 24-72 hours.
  • Rebleeding has high mortality (>50%).
